title: Chapter 1
html:
Foreword

The Western Canon, in its most vital sense, is composed not only of the great texts that endure but also of those that refuse to conform, that antagonize the expectation of unity and frustrate the reader’s desire for conventional meaning. The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman, by Laurence Sterne, stands among that rarest class of literary productions—books that are less novels than anti-novels, less sequences of events than perpetual eruptions of wit, melancholy, satire, and the insatiable drive of consciousness seeking form and ever eluding it. It is not so much a book to be read as a labyrinth to be wandered, a testament to the power of digression, contingency, and the sublime comedy of human striving.

Written in the brittle yet fecund years between 1759 and 1767, amidst the Enlightenment’s apotheosis of reason and empiricism, Sterne’s wild, laughing book arrives like a discordant symphony amidst a rationalist parade. The eighteenth century, that age of polished wit and Johnsonian gravity, of Locke and Hume and Gibbon’s majestic scorn, would not seem fertile soil for such a literary mushroom to sprout, dazzling and toxic in equal measure. But Sterne was not of the Enlightenment; he was its court jester and deconstructionist. He held up the mirror of language to reason and showed the contorted, delightful mask that grinned back. Like Shakespeare before him, Sterne recognized that the mind is never sequential, never obedient to causality. Instead, it is a flickering theater of contradiction, memory, mischief, and grief.

The birth of the novel in English, often attributed to Defoe, Richardson, and Fielding, gains in Sterne a paradoxical culmination and rejection. If Defoe bequeathed the verisimilitude of fact, Richardson the interior monologue of moral progress, and Fielding the comic epic in prose, then Sterne dismantles them all with surgical hilarity. He mocks the narrative arc even as he invokes it; he interrupts himself endlessly, gleefully, as if the very act of finishing a thought were a concession to logic too grievous to endure. This is not progress in the novel’s history—it is sabotage, gloriously wrought. That Sterne’s work was an immense success in his own time, even as it bewildered, is a tribute to the hunger for a different kind of literature, one that dared to embrace the irrationality of being with the arms of artifice and irony.

Sterne’s aesthetic is closer to that of the baroque than the classical, more akin to Rabelais than to Addison. He revels in excess, in the long tail of thought that drags behind the immediate subject. The page becomes a playground for typography, blankness, and blackness—a field of experimentation centuries ahead of postmodernism. Indeed, one cannot but read Tristram Shandy without thinking of the formal innovations of Joyce, the metafictional games of Nabokov, or the ontological riddles of Borges. Yet Sterne’s genius lies not merely in having anticipated later complexities but in having done so with such humane mirth, such bottomless charm.

The book resists synopsis because it mocks the very idea of coherence. It opens not with a birth, but with its absurd prelude; it flirts with the idea of being about Tristram, then flees; it gives us instead a gallery of voices, obsessions, and mannerisms: Uncle Toby with his wars and hobbyhorses, Walter Shandy and his philosophical futilities, the indelible Corporal Trim. Each becomes more than a character; they are philosophies incarnate, caricatures elevated to archetype, men of thought who stumble on the grammar of the body. Sterne's people are not mimetic representations but allegorical grotesques—yet they are beloved because they reflect the fragmentation within us all.

The genius of Sterne lies not in his rejection of form but in his awareness that form is the prisonhouse of meaning. He turns narrative structure inside out and reveals it to be a conceit, an imposition on the chaos of experience. His interruptions are meditations; his footnotes and prefaces are masks behind which the author laughs and weeps. Behind every jest is a sadness, behind every obscenity a spiritual yearning. That Sterne was a clergyman matters deeply. His skepticism was not corrosive but confessional. In his absurdities lies a mourning for lost innocence, for the body’s decay and the futility of aspiration. The comedy is a veil for metaphysical desolation.

One might say that Sterne rewrites Montaigne in the key of parody, that he discovers in Locke’s empiricism the seeds of absurdity. Tristram Shandy is what happens when the self-consciousness of thought begins to eat its own tail. Sterne’s hero cannot be born properly because the self, in its essence, is never complete, always postponed. This is a novel that dramatizes not events, but the impossibility of narrating the self with finality. It is the book of the provisional, the gloriously unfinished, the perpetual deferral. In this, Sterne aligns himself less with his contemporaries than with that deepest of literary instincts—the recognition, in Proust’s phrase, that “the true book is not the one we read but the one we dream.”

The audacity of Sterne’s prose should not be underestimated. His sentences are whirlwinds of parenthesis, dashes, italicizations, and sudden apostrophes. The eighteenth-century novel often glided on neoclassical restraint; Sterne’s language gallops and buckles, as if syntax were itself possessed by the tumult of feeling. His rhythms are both manic and elegiac, a paradox mirrored in the Shandean philosophy itself: that life, however absurd, must be adored. There is in Sterne the sublime melancholy of Hamlet crossed with the slapstick of Punch. Indeed, Yorick—the parson and alter ego who appears within the book—is the shadow of Shakespeare’s skull-bearing fool, meditating on death with a wink. Sterne’s text, like the graveyard scene, is both elegy and jest, and thus strikes the deepest chords.

If we place Sterne beside the major literary forces of the Western tradition, he stands as a singular destabilizer. He mocks the philosophical gravity of his age with a feather’s touch, yet that mockery is never nihilistic. His is a joyous demolition, closer in spirit to Cervantes than to Voltaire. Cervantes gave us the mad knight whose delusions were nobler than reality; Sterne gives us the narrator whose story cannot be told because the mind cannot stop thinking long enough to proceed. Both authors give us a shattered mirror in which the human condition is reflected. Both remind us that literature is not a mirror of nature but the residue of consciousness encountering itself in words.

In terms of influence, Sterne’s mark is indelible though often subterranean. The Victorian era, with its penchant for moral earnestness and plotted continuity, had little room for him. But he haunts the margins—appearing in Dickens’s comic exaggerations, in George Eliot’s intellectual soliloquies, in the psychological hesitations of James. And then, in the twentieth century, Sterne is resurrected with a vengeance. Joyce, whose Ulysses is the ur-text of modern consciousness, owes Sterne an unpayable debt. So too does Beckett, whose characters likewise stammer and stumble beneath the weight of awareness. And Nabokov’s Sebastian Knight, in his vanishing act of autobiographical elusion, is a Shandean ghost. The postmodernists—Barth, Pynchon, Calvino—could not exist without Sterne’s blueprint for play, interruption, and epistemological jest.

But influence, though a useful barometer of canonical status, is not the final arbiter of greatness. What compels us to return to Tristram Shandy is not its innovation alone but its refusal to capitulate to closure. Sterne teaches us that identity is not a substance but a syntax—fragmentary, comic, and poignant. In our present era, obsessed as it is with the performance of selfhood, the curated narrative, and the demand for legibility, Sterne offers a liberating disorder. He reminds us that our lives are not stories with neat resolutions but webs of memory, accident, and longing. His is a radical humanism in comic form.

If we speak of canonical endurance, then Sterne’s novel survives precisely because it is not reducible to theme or plot. Like the best of art, it resists paraphrase. One does not read Tristram Shandy for what happens but for how it unfolds—or more precisely, how it refuses to unfold. It is the novel as jazz improvisation, as fugue and fantasy. It defies the very expectations it conjures and thereby achieves a higher fidelity to experience. The great works of literature do not reflect reality; they deepen it. Sterne deepens our sense of the comic and the tragic as conjoined twins.

In the end, what Sterne accomplishes is a philosophical achievement in literary form: the dramatization of contingency as the essence of being. Life, he suggests, is not a tale to be told but a torrent to be waded. Its beauty lies in its digressions, its meaning in its interruptions. There is more truth in the errant footnote than in the tidy conclusion. To read Sterne is to be reminded that the self is a project never finished, that literature is not a vehicle for resolution but a mirror for our own dazzling incoherence.

In the Cathedral of Western Literature, Sterne does not occupy the pulpit or the altar but the pulpit’s shadow, grinning behind the curtain, scribbling rude annotations in the margins of sermons. He is, perhaps, the guardian of literary misrule—the priest of deviation and the sage of semantic delight. And yet, behind the giggle and the gasp, one senses a spirit deeply attuned to mortality. Sterne’s digressions are not evasions; they are elegies. In the endless play of language, he locates the sob that follows laughter. Tristram Shandy endures because it tells us, in the language of laughter, how tragic it is to be alive, and how beautiful.
